facts_disclosed: Fragrance line cancelled — off the table

turns.content: I need to talk through a business decision I have made. It was not easy, but it is done, and I want to be certain my reasoning is sound.
Of course. Walk me through it — what's the decision, and what led you to it?

I had been developing a home fragrance line for the past several months. It was something I was genuinely excited about — a collection called Oud and Geometry, pairing traditional oud-based scents with what I described as architectural scent profiles. Clean, structural notes layered over the richness of oud. It felt like a natural extension of the brand.
That's an evocative concept — the idea of translating architectural principles into fragrance is unusual and sophisticated. It sounds like it had real potential as a brand extension. So what changed?

Reality changed. I spent four months on it — meetings with perfumers in Grasse, packaging design, market research, cost projections. And what I found was that launching a fragrance line at the level of quality I would demand is essentially building a second business. It requires its own supply chain, its own regulatory compliance, its own distribution network. I was designing a distraction and calling it a brand extension.

That's a very honest assessment. The gap between "brand extension" and "entirely separate business" is one that catches a lot of companies off guard. The operational complexity of fragrance — formulation, compliance, retail distribution — is genuinely a different world from design services. What made you pull the trigger on stopping it rather than scaling it down or partnering with someone?

Because I do not do things at half measure. If I were to put my name on a fragrance, it would need to be exceptional. And exceptional in that category requires a level of investment and attention that would directly compete with my core work. I am an interior designer. That is what the firm does. That is what we are known for. I looked at the next twelve months and asked myself a very simple question: do I want to spend my time negotiating with perfumers and packaging manufacturers, or do I want to spend it designing spaces? The answer was immediate.

That clarity is valuable. The question you asked yourself — where do I want to spend my time — cuts through all the strategic rationalisation and gets to the core of it. Plenty of design firms have diluted their brand by chasing adjacent categories. Did you have any financial commitments already in place that made the decision harder?

Some. I had commissioned initial fragrance development from a house in Grasse — three base compositions. That cost is sunk. I also had preliminary packaging concepts from a design studio in Milan. Perhaps forty thousand riyals in total, committed and unrecoverable. It stings, but it is a fraction of what the full launch would have cost. Better to absorb a small loss now than to bleed resources for the next two years on something that is not our core competence.

That's disciplined thinking. Forty thousand riyals in sunk costs against the potential drain of a multi-year launch effort — that's a clear cost-benefit calculation. The sunk cost fallacy traps a lot of entrepreneurs into continuing projects precisely because they've already invested. The fact that you can walk away from that investment without looking back speaks to real business clarity. Have you communicated the decision to everyone involved?

I have. The perfumers in Grasse were understanding — they are accustomed to projects that do not proceed. The Milan studio was slightly disappointed but professional. And my team here — I told them last week. Some of them were invested in the idea emotionally. But they also understand that focus is not a limitation, it is a strategy. We are not a lifestyle brand. We are a design firm. Oud and Geometry is finished. Forget about it. It is completely off the table.

It sounds like you've handled the wind-down cleanly and communicated with conviction. That last point — "focus is not a limitation, it is a strategy" — is worth holding onto. The most successful firms tend to be the ones that are very clear about what they are and what they are not. Is there any part of the fragrance work that might feed back into your design practice? The scent research, the sensory design thinking?

That is an interesting question. I had not considered it from that angle. The olfactory research did sharpen my thinking about multi-sensory design — how a space is experienced not only through the eyes but through every sense. I have always believed that, but the fragrance work gave me a more disciplined vocabulary for it. I may incorporate scent specification into my design presentations going forward — recommending specific fragrance profiles for rooms, even if I am not producing the fragrances myself. The knowledge is not wasted. Only the commercial venture is.

That's an elegant resolution — the project dies as a product line but lives on as enhanced design thinking. Incorporating scent specification into your presentations would actually be quite distinctive in the interior design field. Most designers stop at visual and tactile; adding an olfactory layer positions you as someone thinking about the complete sensory experience of a space.

You could even frame it to clients as part of your design service — curated scent recommendations that complement the material palette. The credibility of having done serious fragrance research supports it without requiring you to manufacture anything.

Yes. The research becomes a resource rather than a liability. That reframing is useful. I will say — the hardest part was telling my team. Two of them had been working on the packaging concepts in their own time, after hours. They believed in it. I could see the disappointment.

That's the part of leadership that doesn't get talked about enough — making a decision you know is right for the business while watching people who invested emotionally in the other path process that loss. The fact that they were working on it after hours tells you they're passionate, which is a quality you want to keep. How did you frame it for them?

I was direct. I told them the fragrance project is not happening — it is done, it is not coming back, and I need everyone's energy redirected to the work that defines this firm. But I also told them I saw their effort, and that the design sensibility they brought to the packaging was excellent. The skill is not lost. The application simply changes. They understood. They are professionals.

That's well handled — clear and final on the decision, but generous in acknowledging the work. It validates the people without validating the project, which is exactly the right distinction. And redirecting their energy rather than dismissing it keeps the team motivated. The logic holds well from every angle. You identified a distraction, quantified the sunk cost, cut cleanly, and found a way to retain the valuable parts of the work. That's sound decision-making.

Discipline in design. Discipline in business. They are the same muscle. I appreciate you helping me think through this. The decision was already made — I am not someone who revisits a closed matter — but it is valuable to confirm that the reasoning is sound from the outside as well. Thank you.
The reasoning is very sound. And the fact that you're not someone who revisits a closed matter is probably one of the reasons your firm runs as well as it does. You're welcome.
